summary: American video artist, a central figure in the development of video as a fine art medium. Known for slow-motion, large-scale video installations exploring themes of birth, death, and transcendence, often drawing on Christian and Buddhist imagery. Key works include Martyrs (commissioned for St. Paul's Cathedral), The Crossing, and his video designs for Peter Sellars' production of Tristan und Isolde. Also recreated Renaissance paintings (e.g., Pontormo's The Visitation) as video pieces.

works.name: Martyrs (Earth, Air, Fire, Water) (2014)
works.description: Four vertical plasma screens installed in St. Paul's Cathedral, London, each showing a figure soundlessly succumbing to one of the four elements. The first permanent video artwork commissioned by the cathedral. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Four vertical screens showing people succumbing to the four elements, installed in St. Paul's Cathedral
examples: In this building, four vertical screens each show people soundlessly succumbing to one of the four elements. Bill Viola's Martyrs was commissioned for this building

works.name: Tristan und Isolde (with Peter Sellars, 2005)
works.description: Video designs for Peter Sellars' staging of Wagner's opera, praised for bringing 'the Californian forest into the theatre.' A landmark collaboration between opera and video art.

clues.clue: Co-produced with Peter Sellars; praised for 'bringing the Californian forest into the theatre'
examples: This man praised Bill Viola's ability to 'to bring the Californian forest into the theatre' as part of their co-production of Tristan und Isolde.

works.name: The Greeting (1995) / Pontormo recreations
works.description: Bill Viola recreated Pontormo's The Visitation as a slow-motion video piece in which a pregnant woman whispers 'Can you help me?' to an older woman. Part of a broader practice of translating Renaissance paintings into video. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Recreated Pontormo's The Visitation as a video piece with a pregnant woman whispering 'Can you help me?' to an older woman
examples: Bill Viola recreated a painting by this artist as a video piece in which a pregnant woman whispers, 'Can you help me?' to an older woman.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: How Bill Viola is identified and biographical clues.

clues.clue: American video artist; pioneer of video as a fine art medium alongside Nam June Paik
Works explore themes of birth, death, consciousness, and transcendence using extreme slow motion

comprehensive_summary:
Bill Viola (1951-2024) was an American video artist widely regarded as one of the most important artists working in electronic media. His large-scale video installations, architectural video installations, and sound environments explore the themes of human consciousness, birth, death, and transcendence, often in extreme slow motion.

In quiz bowl, Viola appears very rarely as an answerline but surfaces as a mention in several questions. His most concrete clue is Martyrs (Earth, Air, Fire, Water), a 2014 work permanently installed in St. Paul's Cathedral, London, consisting of four vertical screens showing figures succumbing to the four classical elements. This work appeared in a question about St. Paul's Cathedral at 2018 ACF Regionals.

His collaboration with opera director Peter Sellars on Wagner's Tristan und Isolde (2005) is another identifiable clue, where Sellars praised Viola's ability to 'bring the Californian forest into the theatre.' This appeared in a 2019 question about Peter Sellars.

Viola also recreated Renaissance paintings as video works, most notably Pontormo's The Visitation, reimagined as a piece in which a pregnant woman whispers 'Can you help me?' to an older woman. This appeared in a 2021 bonus leadin about Pontormo.

Viola is often contextualized alongside Nam June Paik as a pioneer of video art, though Paik is far more frequently asked about in quiz bowl. Viola's work draws heavily on Christian mysticism, Zen Buddhism, and Sufi traditions, and his pieces are held by major museums worldwide including the Getty, MoMA, and the Whitney.
